rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit via the parent statement: "Implement adaptation actions related to water and wastewater management to reduce exposure and vulnerability" (explicit_parent). The measure itself is resource-efficiency/water-efficiency: "Old water distribution network will be replaced... to minimize the leakages" and aims to "save desalinated potable water" and "avoid... new desalination plants," indicating reduced water losses and reduced need for energy-intensive desalination. The gate failure (missing token) appears to be a false negative because the text directly describes leak reduction and efficiency gains.
policy_statement: Replace old potable water distribution networks with new networks to minimize leakages and avoid increased desalination capacity needs.
mechanism_description: Replacing old potable water distribution networks with new networks reduces leaks, saving desalinated potable water and avoiding the need to increase or add desalination plant capacity.
primary_causal_pathway: Same water service → ↓ water losses → ↓ desalination + pumping energy → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Utilities replace/rehabilitate aging potable water distribution pipes and fittings (and often improve pressure management) to cut leakage/non-revenue water.  
(2) Process: Lower physical water losses mean less potable water must be produced; where supply is desalination, reduced set output defers/avoids desalination plant expansion and reduces ongoing desalination and conveyance energy use.  
(3) Outcome: Lower electricity/thermal fuel consumption for desalination and pumping leads to reduced lifecycle GHG emissions (and can also reduce peak power demand).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing energy demand (and thus GHG emissions) by avoiding the need to desalinate and pump additional potable water to compensate for distribution losses.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: delivering the same potable water service with less water production by reducing leaks, which reduces desalination/pumping energy and associated GHGs.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while avoided capacity expansion is a planning benefit, the climate-relevant effect is fundamentally efficiency—less energy input per unit of delivered water—so Resource Efficiency best fits the dominant mechanism.  
3) Edge cases applied: if desalination is powered by near-zero-carbon energy, mitigation benefits shrink but the mechanism still operates as resource/energy efficiency; if the policy’s main intent were drought resilience (reliability under shortages), it could support adaptation as a co-benefit, but not as the dominant pathway stated here.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: any policy that reduces distribution leakage to avoid additional desalination capacity necessarily reduces required desalinated water production and associated energy use, preserving the Resource Efficiency classification across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether the realized benefit is primarily operational energy savings vs avoided new plant construction depends on local grid carbon intensity, baseline leakage rates, and supply mix, and should be determined at the individual policy-evidence level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the additional water would have come from low-energy sources (e.g., gravity-fed surface water) rather than desalination, the same leak-reduction mechanism still fits Resource Efficiency but with different magnitude; if framed/implemented chiefly for continuity of supply during climate-driven droughts, adaptation could be a secondary co-benefit rather than the primary category here.
key_indicators: "Old water distribution network will be replaced with a new water distribution network"; "minimize the leakages"; "save desalinated potable water"; "avoiding increasing the production capacity of the existing plants"; "avoiding the need for new desalination plants"
co_benefits: Improved water service reliability/pressure; reduced non-revenue water and utility operating costs; reduced risk of contamination from pipe breaks/leaks; enhanced drought resilience through demand reduction.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a utility planning/asset-management or performance-target approach if the policy text specified NRW targets/standards; however, the verbatim text centers on network replacement (capital works), so infrastructure-investment is the clearest fit.
